insights.insight: Verified receipt data settles attribution at ~$0.10 per confirmed purchase event versus $5–$150 CPA for modeled conversions in standard ad tech — the gap exists because a cryptographically signed receipt eliminates the attribution problem rather than solving it probabilistically.

With Chrome completing cookie deprecation and device fingerprinting increasingly blocked, the ad industry has no cross-ecosystem, identity-free purchase signal at scale — verified receipt infrastructure is one of the few mechanisms that produces deterministic conversion data without requiring PII.

Amazon knows Amazon spend, Kroger knows Kroger spend — neither sees the full wallet; a cross-ecosystem receipt layer captures both simultaneously without identity attachment, which is the specific information gap that no first-party data program can close by definition.

The network is expanding across non-metro CBSAs in 15+ states, capturing a consumer population that is systematically underrepresented in cookie-based targeting and first-party data programs — rural spend is real but largely invisible to digital ad infrastructure.

Current brand coverage spans grocery (Kroger, H-E-B, King Soopers), mass retail (Walmart, Target), warehouse (Costco), and home improvement (Home Depot), meaning the receipt signal crosses category lines that no single retailer's loyalty program can provide.

Token velocity has held range-bound for 6+ consecutive measurement windows, suggesting consistent intake rather than speculative or event-driven spikes — for brands evaluating data infrastructure, flat velocity with geographic expansion is a more credible baseline than growth driven by anomalous events.
